concept: Push / Press Away / Move by Force from the Body
description: To push: the boat from the shore, the cart up the lane, the door that sticks. Force directed away from oneself. The exact opposite of 'natu' (pull) — the pair share no sounds, so direction of force is always unmistakable.
sound_symbolism: The 'p' bursts outward from closed lips — force leaving the body — and 'sa' carries it away in a hiss of motion. Effort, outbound.
grammatical_notes: Transitive: 'mia ponu pesa.' (I push the door.) 'lo mia lopha pesa.' (We push the vessel.) With effort marked: 'shia ru kema pesa.' (They push with great strength.) Opposite: 'natu' (pull) — the pair share no sounds.

pillars.solarpunk-values: Human-scale force — the pushed cart, the pushed plough — is the renewable engine; the word dignifies muscle-work as technology.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: The plough, the cart, the mill-arm: most old work was pushing something patient through something resistant.
